I.A.1.text: Compare the scope of WCAG (2.0, 2.1, 2.2), ATAG 2.0, and EN 301 549, and identify how each set of requirements influences web accessibility design.
I.A.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.2.text: Explain the structure of WCAG 2.2 by identifying the four principles (POUR), guidelines, and success criteria levels (A, AA, AAA).
I.A.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.3.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative parts of WCAG and justify why that distinction matters for conformance.
I.A.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.4.text: Apply W3C-recommended techniques (e.g., sufficient techniques, failure techniques) to specific WCAG success criteria in real coding scenarios.
I.A.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.5.text: Summarize the rationale for using the latest WCAG 2.2 and evaluate the impact of new success criteria (e.g., Focus Not Obscured) on website design.
I.A.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.6.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative parts of WAI-ARIA and justify why that distinction matters for conformance.
I.A.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.7.text: Explain the purpose and impact of WAI-ARIA 1.2 on web accessibility
I.A.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.8.text: Describe the WAI-ARIA 1.2 model of roles, states, and properties
I.A.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.9.text: Determine when to use WAI-ARIA 1.2 versus standard HTML elements
I.A.9.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.10.text: Apply the five rules for using ARIA correctly
I.A.10.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.11.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative parts of ATAG and justify why that distinction matters for conformance.
I.A.11.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.12.text: Explain how ATAG 2.0 applies to web content authoring tools
I.A.12.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.13.text: Describe the meaning and intent of the two main sections of ATAG 2.0 (Part A: Accessible user interface; Part B: Supporting accessible content production)
I.A.13.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.14.text: Distinguish between automated accessibility practices in authoring tools and practices requiring author/user input
I.A.14.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.15.text: Evaluate the power and limitations of automated accessibility authoring features
I.A.15.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.16.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative information in ATAG 2.0
I.A.16.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.17.text: Identify the types of technologies addressed by EN 301 549
I.A.17.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.18.text: Compare and contrast the similarities and differences between WCAG and EN 301 549
I.A.18.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.19.text: Explain how WCAG is represented in EN 301 549
I.A.19.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.20.text: Identify requirements beyond WCAG that are relevant for compliance with the Web Accessibility Directive 2016/2102
I.A.20.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.21.text: Interpret the self-scoping (applicability) language used in EN 301 549
I.A.21.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.A.22.text: Explain the intent and scope of EN 301 549 requirements that extend beyond WCAG
I.A.22.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.1.text: Demonstrate how JavaScript event handlers can be made device-independent (e.g., supporting both pointer and keyboard input).
I.B.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.2.text: Explain progressive enhancement and outline the implications of turning JavaScript off or using older browsers for accessibility.
I.B.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.3.text: Identify common pitfalls in dynamic content updates (e.g., partial page refreshes with AJAX) and implement focus management or live regions to keep screen reader users informed.
I.B.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.4.text: Distinguish between semantic HTML controls (e.g., `<button>`, `<a>`) and generic elements (e.g., `<div>`) in terms of built-in accessibility.
I.B.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.5.text: Analyze how focus order, event handling (onClick, onKeyDown, etc.), and ARIA states can either improve or harm the user experience for assistive technology.
I.B.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.6.text: Analyze how JavaScript implementations can either improve or reduce accessibility and usability for people using assistive technologies.
I.B.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.7.text: Compare the accessibility implications of applying JavaScript event handlers (such as onclick) to semantic elements (e.g., `<a>` or `<button>`) versus non-semantic elements (e.g., `<div>`).
I.B.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.B.8.text: Determine appropriate focus management techniques for moving focus to newly revealed or dynamically generated content.
I.B.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.1.text: Construct a custom widget (e.g., a modal dialog or accordion) using the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices, ensuring correct roles, states, and properties.
I.C.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.2.text: Demonstrate correct keyboard interaction models (e.g., tab vs. arrow keys) for composite components such as menus, tabs, and disclosure widgets.
I.C.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.3.text: Evaluate existing custom controls for missing or incorrect ARIA markup, and recommend solutions to align them with best practices.
I.C.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.4.text: Analyze accessibility of interactive components and ARIA-based widgets based on expected keyboard and screen reader behavior.
I.C.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.5.text: Analyze interactive components to ensure they support single-pointer alternatives for gesture-based interactions.
I.C.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.6.text: Apply established keyboard interaction conventions when testing ARIA-based custom widgets.
I.C.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.7.text: Apply established keyboard interaction models when implementing ARIA-based custom widgets, including the use of Tab to enter widgets and arrow keys to navigate within them.
I.C.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.8.text: Apply standard keyboard interaction models when designing and implementing ARIA-based custom widgets.
I.C.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.9.text: Evaluate accessibility of interactive components based on keyboard navigation and screen reader behavior.
I.C.9.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.10.text: Evaluate implementations of custom widgets to determine whether they follow established accessibility authoring practices.
I.C.10.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.C.11.text: Evaluate when native HTML controls should be used instead of custom ARIA widgets based on accessibility best practices and implementation complexity.
I.C.11.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.1.text: Explain the five primary rules of ARIA usage (e.g., "use native HTML first," "do not change native semantics unless necessary").
I.D.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.2.text: Implement accessible names and descriptions using `aria-label`, `aria-labelledby`, and `aria-describedby`, and justify which approach is most appropriate in a given scenario.
I.D.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.3.text: Demonstrate correct focus management using `aria-activedescendant` or roving `tabindex` in interactive widgets.
I.D.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.4.text: Analyze how the accessibility tree is exposed to assistive technology and verify ARIA roles, states, and properties in different screen reader/browser combinations.
I.D.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.5.text: Analyze accessibility implications of complex web applications and determine how ARIA roles, states, and properties can communicate interface changes to assistive technologies.
I.D.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.6.text: Analyze web interface patterns to determine when WAI-ARIA is appropriate.
I.D.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.7.text: Analyze widget implementations to determine whether appropriate WAI-ARIA roles, states, and properties are applied correctly.
I.D.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.8.text: Apply ARIA live region properties to announce dynamically added content without moving keyboard focus when appropriate.
I.D.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.9.text: Apply ARIA properties and states to communicate widget state and dynamic changes in user interfaces (e.g., checked, expanded).
I.D.9.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.10.text: Apply techniques for providing accessible names and descriptions to interactive controls and widgets.
I.D.10.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.11.text: Compare the accessibility benefits and limitations of native HTML controls and custom WAI-ARIA widgets.
I.D.11.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.12.text: Describe the semantic structure of ARIA roles, including required parent roles, child roles, and associated attributes.
I.D.12.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.13.text: Describe the WAI-ARIA roles, states, and properties model and how it communicates semantics to assistive technologies.
I.D.13.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.14.text: Determine appropriate use of ARIA live regions to notify assistive technologies of dynamic content updates.
I.D.14.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.15.text: Evaluate different techniques for notifying assistive technologies when dynamic content changes occur, including the use of ARIA live regions and related mechanisms.
I.D.15.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.16.text: Evaluate scenarios where the application role may be used and justify when it should be avoided due to its impact on assistive technology keyboard navigation.
I.D.16.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.17.text: Evaluate whether native HTML semantics or WAI-ARIA should be used in a given implementation scenario.
I.D.17.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.18.text: Explain how assigning an ARIA role overrides the native semantic role of an HTML element and the implications this has for accessibility APIs and assistive technologies.
I.D.18.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.19.text: Explain the five rules for using ARIA and their importance in accessible interface design.
I.D.19.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.20.text: Explain the purpose of WAI-ARIA and its role in improving accessibility for dynamic web content and custom interface components.
I.D.20.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.21.text: Explain the role of the accessibility tree in conveying semantic information from web content to assistive technologies.
I.D.21.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.D.22.text: Identify appropriate ARIA roles used to describe interactive widgets (e.g., menu, treeitem, slider, progressbar) and structural elements of a page (e.g., headings, regions, tables).
I.D.22.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.1.text: Identify how assistive technologies (e.g., screen readers, magnifiers, voice input) interact with browsers and what "accessibility-supported" means under WCAG.
I.E.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.2.text: Assess the compatibility of a chosen technology stack (front-end framework, JS library) with major screen reader/browser pairs and recommend fallback or polyfill strategies.
I.E.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.3.text: Explain how touch interaction changes with a screen reader turned on (e.g., iOS VoiceOver gestures) and demonstrate design patterns to support these gestures.
I.E.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.4.text: Analyze accessibility issues to determine whether they result from design or implementation flaws versus limitations in browser or assistive technology support.
I.E.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.5.text: Distinguish between accessibility features that can be implemented automatically by authoring tools and those requiring author input.
I.E.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.6.text: Evaluate the capabilities and limitations of automated accessibility support within authoring tools.
I.E.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.7.text: Evaluate unexpected screen reader behavior to determine whether it results from accessibility issues in the web content, limitations in assistive technology support, or incorrect testing techniques.
I.E.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.E.8.text: Explain how ARIA can enhance assistive technology support for dynamic content and custom user interface components.
I.E.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.F.1.text: Compare native HTML controls' built-in accessibility features with custom (ARIA-based) components, highlighting pros and cons.
I.F.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.F.2.text: Demonstrate how to repurpose a non-semantic element into a fully accessible custom button or link using proper ARIA attributes, keyboard events, and visible focus.
I.F.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.F.3.text: Recommend standard HTML controls whenever possible and justify the business and user benefits of reducing custom code for accessibility.
I.F.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.1.text: Demonstrate how to manage announcements of dynamic page updates (e.g., with `aria-live`) when navigation or content changes do not trigger a full page reload.
I.G.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.2.text: Implement focus changes appropriately when new views or dialogs appear, ensuring screen-reader and keyboard-only users can track SPA updates.
I.G.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.3.text: Evaluate the user experience of a single-page application with typical assistive technologies and propose solutions for any discovered gaps.
I.G.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.4.text: Analyze dynamic content updates in SPAs to determine whether users should be notified when new content is loaded.
I.G.4.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.5.text: Evaluate touch interfaces to confirm that touch targets are large enough and sufficiently spaced.
I.G.5.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.6.text: Explain accessibility challenges associated with single-page applications (SPAs), including focus management, dynamic content updates, and assistive technology compatibility.
I.G.6.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.7.text: Test single-page application behaviors with screen readers to verify that asynchronous content updates are correctly announced and accessible.
I.G.7.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.G.8.text: Test whether focus indicators, labels, and instructions remain visible and usable at increased zoom levels or modified text spacing.
I.G.8.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.H.1.text: Describe key strategies that users with blindness, low vision, motor disabilities, auditory disabilities, and cognitive/learning disabilities employ to interact with web content.
I.H.1.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.H.2.text: Analyze design elements (e.g., headings, landmarks, color contrast) to determine their impact on diverse user groups' navigation and comprehension strategies.
I.H.2.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

I.H.3.text: Propose layout or structural revisions (e.g., skip links, consistent navigation) that accommodate multiple disability groups.
I.H.3.domain_title: Domain I: Creating Accessible Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.1.text: Identify potential issues arising from specific browser–screen reader combinations (e.g., JAWS + Chrome vs. NVDA + Firefox).
II.A.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.2.text: Explain why certain ARIA roles or HTML5 elements might not be fully supported across older browsers, and how to mitigate these gaps.
II.A.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.3.text: Evaluate a website's accessibility across multiple devices (desktop, tablet, mobile) and assistive technologies.
II.A.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.4.text: Analyze how accessibility support changes when assistive technologies are combined with different user agents.
II.A.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.5.text: Analyze how users with disabilities interact with web content using assistive technologies, adaptive strategies, and user agents.
II.A.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.6.text: Describe how assistive technologies interact with user agents, including browsers and applications.
II.A.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.A.7.text: Explain how web content, user agents (browsers and media players), assistive technologies, and authoring tools interact to provide an accessible web experience.
II.A.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.1.text: Map discovered accessibility barriers to the appropriate WCAG success criterion (or EN 301 549 clause) and distinguish conformance failures from best-practice gaps.
II.B.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.2.text: Apply the WCAG-EM methodology to define scope, explore site content, select a representative sample, and evaluate conformance thoroughly.
II.B.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.3.text: Explain the concept of conformance levels (A, AA, AAA) and defend the rationale for choosing a specific level in a project or legal context.
II.B.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.4.text: Describe the limitations in WCAG (e.g., certain gaps for cognitive or motion-based issues) and propose best-practice enhancements.
II.B.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.5.text: Analyze accessibility issues to determine whether they represent failures of WCAG Success Criteria or broader usability or accessibility concerns not explicitly covered by the specifications.
II.B.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.6.text: Analyze differences in how assistive technology users may interact with web content based on skill level, navigation strategies, and personal preferences.
II.B.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.7.text: Analyze dynamic content updates to determine when keyboard focus should be moved to newly loaded content.
II.B.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.8.text: Analyze examples of web content to determine whether they meet or fail specific WCAG success criteria.
II.B.8.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.9.text: Analyze the scope and intent of the requirements listed in Annex A, Table A.1 in EN 301 549 that go beyond WCAG.
II.B.9.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.10.text: Apply standard keyboard interaction patterns to test native HTML interactive elements.
II.B.10.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.11.text: Apply the WCAG Evaluation Methodology (WCAG-EM) to systematically evaluate the accessibility of web content.
II.B.11.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.12.text: Assess caption accuracy and completeness to ensure that automatic captioning errors have been corrected.
II.B.12.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.13.text: Assess form fields to ensure appropriate autocomplete attributes are implemented where relevant.
II.B.13.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.14.text: Classify WCAG 2.2 Success Criteria according to their conformance levels (A, AA, AAA).
II.B.14.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.15.text: Compare the accessibility requirements in EN 301 549 with those defined in WCAG.
II.B.15.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.16.text: Describe the purpose and scope of the two primary sections of ATAG 2.0.
II.B.16.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.17.text: Describe the relationship between WCAG principles, guidelines, and success criteria.
II.B.17.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.18.text: Differentiate between accessibility issues that can be detected by automated tools and those that require manual evaluation.
II.B.18.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.19.text: Differentiate between sufficient techniques, advisory techniques, and failures associated with WCAG success criteria.
II.B.19.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.20.text: Differentiate between the disciplines of accessibility and user experience (UX) design, including their goals, assumptions, and areas of overlap.
II.B.20.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.21.text: Distinguish between normative and non-normative information within accessibility specifications.
II.B.21.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.22.text: Explain factors that influence the selection of a target accessibility conformance level (e.g., legal requirements, organizational policies, or project goals).
II.B.22.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.23.text: Explain how ATAG 2.0 applies to web content authoring tools and development environments.
II.B.23.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.24.text: Explain how WCAG requirements are incorporated into EN 301 549.
II.B.24.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.25.text: Explain the intent and accessibility impact of each WCAG principle.
II.B.25.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.26.text: Explain WCAG conformance requirements and how they apply to evaluating web content.
II.B.26.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.27.text: Identify additional accessibility requirements in EN 301 549 that extend beyond WCAG.
II.B.27.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.28.text: Identify relevant accessibility requirements in specifications such as WCAG 2.2, WAI-ARIA, and ATAG when evaluating accessibility issues.
II.B.28.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.29.text: Identify the technologies and digital products addressed by EN 301 549.
II.B.29.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.30.text: Identify WCAG 2.2 Success Criteria and their associated conformance levels (A, AA, AAA).
II.B.30.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.31.text: Interpret the intent and requirements of ATAG principles, guidelines, and success criteria.
II.B.31.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.32.text: Interpret the requirements of WCAG success criteria and their implications for accessible design and development.
II.B.32.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.B.33.text: Summarize the major additions introduced in WCAG 2.1, particularly those addressing new input methods and improved support for low vision and cognitive disabilities.
II.B.33.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.1.text: Conduct screen reader testing to confirm that name, role, value, and state changes of interactive elements are conveyed properly.
II.C.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.2.text: Demonstrate manual keyboard testing to detect missing focus indicators, incorrect tab order, or keyboard traps.
II.C.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.3.text: Use magnifier or high-contrast settings to detect any text or interface elements that become unusable or unreadable.
II.C.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.4.text: Identify common user needs for auditory disabilities (e.g., accurate captions, transcripts) and evaluate if they are met.
II.C.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.5.text: Evaluate focus management, focus order, and visibility of focus indicators during keyboard navigation.
II.C.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.6.text: Evaluate form implementations based on expected screen reader interaction patterns.
II.C.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.7.text: Evaluate HTML markup to verify that semantic structure (e.g., headings, lists, tables, and landmarks) correctly conveys meaning to assistive technologies.
II.C.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.8.text: Evaluate keyboard operability, focus order, and link descriptions to ensure they support effective screen reader navigation.
II.C.8.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.9.text: Evaluate navigation structures, headings, and labels to determine whether they provide clear and consistent information architecture.
II.C.9.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.10.text: Evaluate responsive behavior to ensure content adapts to different viewport sizes without requiring two-dimensional scrolling.
II.C.10.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.11.text: Evaluate scenarios where it may be appropriate to supplement or override browser or assistive technology behavior to improve accessibility, while considering potential risks and compatibility impacts.
II.C.11.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.12.text: Evaluate the limitations of accessibility guidelines, principles, and techniques when assessing real-world accessibility issues.
II.C.12.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.13.text: Evaluate web content to determine whether text alternatives are provided for images, video, and other non-text content.
II.C.13.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.14.text: Evaluate web implementations to ensure accessibility solutions follow established web standards rather than relying on technology-specific quirks.
II.C.14.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.15.text: Test character-key shortcuts to ensure they can be disabled, remapped, or activated only when the relevant component has focus.
II.C.15.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.16.text: Test dynamic content updates to confirm that roles, names, values, and status messages are communicated to screen readers using appropriate ARIA attributes or live regions.
II.C.16.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.17.text: Test the reading order of web content to confirm that the programmatic order is meaningful and consistent with the intended visual presentation.
II.C.17.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.18.text: Test web interfaces to confirm that all functionality can be operated using a keyboard without keyboard traps.
II.C.18.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.19.text: Verify that error messages are programmatically associated with form fields and that clear instructions and suggestions for correction are provided.
II.C.19.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.C.20.text: Verify that page language, form errors, and component states are programmatically conveyed to assistive technologies.
II.C.20.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.1.text: Compare the strengths and limitations of automated tools, browser-based linters, spider tools, and bookmarklets for accessibility testing.
II.D.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.2.text: Use at least one site-wide scanning tool and one browser-based developer tool (e.g., Axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) to detect markup or ARIA errors.
II.D.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.3.text: Interpret automated test results to confirm valid failures, eliminate false positives, and plan follow-up manual checks.
II.D.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.4.text: Analyze accessibility issues identified by testing tools and determine appropriate follow-up manual testing strategies.
II.D.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.5.text: Analyze automated accessibility testing results to identify false positives, omissions, and issues requiring manual verification.
II.D.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.6.text: Analyze how accessibility testing tools can be applied at different stages of the web development lifecycle, including design, development, and testing.
II.D.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.7.text: Analyze how accessibility testing tools can be integrated at different stages of the web development lifecycle, including design, development, and testing.
II.D.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.8.text: Evaluate the strengths and limitations of automated accessibility testing tools.
II.D.8.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.9.text: Explain the purpose of Accessibility Conformance Testing (ACT) Rules and their role in standardizing automated accessibility testing.
II.D.9.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.10.text: Identify common categories of accessibility testing tools, including site-wide scanning tools, server-based analysis tools, unit and integration testing tools, browser developer tools, browser extensions, simulators, and guided manual testing tools.
II.D.10.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.11.text: Inspect HTML structure and accessibility attributes using browser developer tools to identify potential accessibility issues.
II.D.11.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.D.12.text: Inspect HTML structure and accessibility-related attributes using browser developer tools.
II.D.12.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.1.text: Explain how accessibility can be integrated into agile vs. waterfall lifecycles, focusing on early detection of issues.
II.E.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.2.text: Establish a continuous testing process (automated + manual) that catches regressions after updates or new releases.
II.E.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.3.text: Coordinate with product owners, designers, and developers to incorporate accessibility checks into acceptance criteria and "definition of done."
II.E.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.4.text: Analyze development workflows to determine how accessibility practices can be incorporated into ongoing quality assurance and regression testing processes.
II.E.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.5.text: Compare Agile and Waterfall project management methodologies and analyze how each approach incorporates accessibility quality assurance activities.
II.E.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.6.text: Explain how accessibility considerations should be integrated throughout the product development lifecycle, including concept, requirements, design, prototyping, development, quality assurance, user testing, support, and regression testing.
II.E.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.E.7.text: Explain how accessibility maturity models and assessment tools can be used to measure progress and support long-term accessibility sustainability.
II.E.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.F.1.text: Demonstrate usage of at least two popular screen reader/browser combinations (e.g., NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari) to confirm consistent user experience.
II.F.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.F.2.text: Verify that dynamically updated or hidden elements are properly announced or hidden from screen readers.
II.F.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.F.3.text: Assess whether alternative input methods (switch control, voice input) can navigate and operate interactive elements effectively.
II.F.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.F.4.text: Evaluate whether interactive elements are implemented using native controls or appropriate ARIA semantics to support voice control and alternative input devices.
II.F.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.F.5.text: Identify issues for users who use keyboard or alternative input devices.
II.F.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.1.text: Perform usability walk-throughs with simulated low-vision settings (zoom, high contrast) to find layout or text-flow issues.
II.G.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.2.text: Assess mobile interaction (touch gestures, orientation changes) to ensure elements remain accessible and do not require two-dimensional scrolling.
II.G.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.3.text: Identify potential cognitive barriers (e.g., complex language or form instructions) and recommend simpler language or multi-step guidance.
II.G.3.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.4.text: Evaluate motion-based interactions to ensure they can be disabled and replaced with accessible controls.
II.G.4.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.5.text: Evaluate multimedia content to verify that captions are provided for video and transcripts are available for audio-only content.
II.G.5.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.6.text: Evaluate the impact of contrast enhancement modes (such as Windows High Contrast Mode) on the presentation and usability of web interfaces.
II.G.6.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.7.text: Evaluate time limits, moving content, and automatically playing media to determine whether users can control or disable them.
II.G.7.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.8.text: Explain how touch-based interactions change when screen readers are enabled on mobile devices.
II.G.8.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.9.text: Identify accessibility issues related to sensory-dependent instructions, automatically playing audio, or moving content that may interfere with screen reader use.
II.G.9.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.10.text: Test device orientation support to ensure content and functionality are available in both portrait and landscape orientations.
II.G.10.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.11.text: Test mobile interfaces to confirm that all functionality available on desktop viewports is also accessible on smaller touch screens.
II.G.11.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.12.text: Test web interfaces for sufficient text and non-text contrast.
II.G.12.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.13.text: Understand the principles of color contrast requirements in web accessibility and evaluate when and how they apply to ensure text and visual elements are perceivable to users with visual impairments.
II.G.13.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.14.text: Verify that gesture-based interactions provide accessible single-pointer alternatives.
II.G.14.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.15.text: Verify that motion-based or device-motion interactions provide accessible alternative controls.
II.G.15.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.G.16.text: Verify that text can be resized up to 200% without loss of functionality or content.
II.G.16.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.H.1.text: Demonstrate how to use color-contrast analyzers, focus inspectors, or code validators to confirm partial compliance with WCAG criteria.
II.H.1.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

II.H.2.text: Explain how ACT Rules can guide consistent test procedures across multiple tools and testers.
II.H.2.domain_title: Domain II: Identify Accessibility Issues in Web Solutions (40%)

III.A.1.text: Categorize accessibility issues based on severity (blockers vs. minor nuisances) and formulate a remediation timeline that balances user impact and resource constraints.
III.A.1.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.2.text: Justify which issues must be addressed first by assessing legal risk, user impact, and overall usability.
III.A.2.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.3.text: Demonstrate how to communicate high-impact barriers (e.g., no keyboard support, missing alt text on key images) to stakeholders and developers.
III.A.3.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.4.text: Analyze accessibility issues to determine their severity based on user impact, legal risk, and implementation effort.
III.A.4.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.5.text: Assess the severity and user impact of accessibility barriers, recognizing that some issues create greater barriers than others.
III.A.5.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.6.text: Evaluate the importance and urgency of dynamically loaded content to determine the most appropriate notification strategy for assistive technology users.
III.A.6.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.A.7.text: Prioritize accessibility remediation tasks using cost–benefit considerations and the potential impact on users with disabilities.
III.A.7.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.1.text: Propose ARIA-based or native HTML solutions for each conformance failure, explaining why a given fix is both accessible and feasible.
III.B.1.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.2.text: Distinguish between "quick fixes" that resolve localized errors and "redesign" approaches that address deeper structural problems.
III.B.2.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.3.text: Collaborate with design and development teams to implement new UI patterns or code libraries that systematically eliminate repeated accessibility faults.
III.B.3.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.4.text: Analyze results from automated and manual testing tools to determine appropriate follow-up testing or remediation steps.
III.B.4.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.5.text: Assess remediation strategies to determine whether an ideal solution or a “good enough” solution is appropriate given project constraints, target users, and available resources.
III.B.5.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.6.text: Communicate remediation strategies, including the purpose, approach, and expected outcomes, to relevant stakeholders.
III.B.6.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.7.text: Communicate the purpose, approach, and expected outcomes of accessibility remediation strategies to relevant stakeholders.
III.B.7.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.8.text: Differentiate between targeted accessibility fixes and broader redesign approaches when addressing accessibility barriers.
III.B.8.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.9.text: Evaluate remediation options to determine whether a targeted fix or a broader redesign is the most appropriate solution.
III.B.9.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.10.text: Evaluate remediation strategies to determine the most practical and widely applicable solution for a given accessibility issue.
III.B.10.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.11.text: Evaluate the benefits of integrating accessibility during design and development compared to addressing accessibility through post-development remediation.
III.B.11.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.12.text: Identify appropriate stakeholders and engage them in planning and implementing accessibility remediation efforts.
III.B.12.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.13.text: Identify key stakeholders involved in accessibility remediation and engage them in planning and implementing solutions.
III.B.13.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.14.text: Incorporate feedback from users with disabilities to validate and improve remediation approaches.
III.B.14.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.15.text: Incorporate feedback from users with disabilities to validate and refine remediation strategies.
III.B.15.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.16.text: Recommend practical remediation techniques that improve accessibility while minimizing disruption to existing systems.
III.B.16.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.B.17.text: Recommend practical techniques that improve accessibility while minimizing disruption to existing systems.
III.B.17.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.1.text: Evaluate vendor Accessibility Conformance Reports (VPAT/ACR) to determine product compatibility with organizational accessibility standards.
III.C.1.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.2.text: Recommend contract language that mandates the vendor to fix accessibility defects within specified timeframes and supply updated VPATs.
III.C.2.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.3.text: Develop a procurement checklist incorporating EN 301 549 or WCAG references to ensure new products and services meet accessibility requirements.
III.C.3.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.4.text: Implement a vendor management process that includes accessibility roadmap reviews, defect remediation, and ongoing conformance monitoring.
III.C.4.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.5.text: Analyze compatibility issues that occur across different combinations of assistive technologies, browsers, and operating systems.
III.C.5.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.6.text: Assess the feasibility of proposed remediation solutions across different project contexts and technical environments.
III.C.6.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.7.text: Compare how assistive technologies behave across different platforms, devices, and operating systems.
III.C.7.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.8.text: Compare interaction methods used by assistive technologies across platforms, including keyboard-based navigation on desktop and gesture-based navigation on mobile devices.
III.C.8.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.9.text: Describe how device-independent event handlers support accessibility across keyboard, mouse, touch, and assistive technology interactions.
III.C.9.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.10.text: Differentiate between types of Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs) and interpret the accessibility information they provide.
III.C.10.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.11.text: Evaluate accessibility claims and documented conformance statements within procurement documentation.
III.C.11.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.12.text: Evaluate design decisions related to selecting technologies that support accessibility across platforms and assistive technologies.
III.C.12.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.13.text: Evaluate web interfaces for interoperability across browsers, platforms, and assistive technologies (e.g., JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, Chrome, Safari).
III.C.13.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.14.text: Explain how accessibility requirements can be incorporated into organizational procurement processes.
III.C.14.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.15.text: Explain the role of accessibility experts in evaluating vendors and advising on accessibility requirements during procurement.
III.C.15.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.16.text: Identify accessibility risks and issues raised during procurement evaluations.
III.C.16.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.17.text: Recommend mitigation strategies to address accessibility gaps identified during the procurement process.
III.C.17.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)

III.C.18.text: Test interactive components for accessibility across multiple browsers, platforms, and assistive technologies.
III.C.18.domain_title: Domain III: Remediating Issues in Web Solutions (20%)
